evidence: Package layout maps directly to domain capabilities: `api` (HTTP routing/handlers), `config` (grammar loading, serialization, data carriers), `exploration` (semantic field algorithms), `indexer` (corpus ingestion), `query` (BlackLab query execution), `model` (shared data types), `utils` (pure utilities). Each boundary is explicable.

API handler decomposition is deliberate: one class per concern slice — SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, plus ExploreResponseAssembler. No god-class handler.
ExplorationService interface insulates the HTTP layer from the concrete SemanticFieldExplorer; WordSketchApiServer accepts ExplorationService injection for testing.

Config subsystem cleanly separates data (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig records) from loading (GrammarConfigLoader) and serialization (GrammarConfigSerializer). Data carriers have no JSON library dependency.

SemanticFieldExplorer Javadoc describes it as a 'coordination facade' that owns single-seed exploration directly and delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator. The stated design intent is documented.
Both `api` and `exploration` packages have package-info.java summarising their responsibility scope.

handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc states 'Also reachable at the legacy path /api/semantic-field' but WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes() registers only /api/semantic-field/compare — the legacy path is never bound. Project reference docs do not match runtime reality here.

SemanticFieldExplorer implements ExplorationService but directly contains the full multi-phase single-seed algorithm (~130 lines across 4 private phase helpers). Its Javadoc describes it as a 'coordination facade' which is accurate for multi-seed and comparison but not for single-seed, where it is the algorithm owner. Two change axes coexist in one class: facade wiring changes and algorithm changes.
CollocateQueryHelper uses a clear two-phase pipeline (collectHits / scoreHits) with well-named private records (CollocateSearch, HitRecord) that make data flow explicit
BlackLabSnippetParser is a focused, side-effect-free utility class: each static method does one thing (trimLeftAtSentenceBoundary, extractCollocateLemma, detokenize) with no shared mutable state

GrammarConfigLoader decomposes parsing into discrete, well-named private helpers (parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation) making the control flow linear and traceable
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling centralises exception-to-HTTP-status mapping in a single wrapper, keeping error handling consistent and local across all endpoints
ExploreResponseAssembler is a pure conversion class with symmetric populateExploreResponse / populateComparisonResponse methods; no HTTP or I/O state leaks in

MultiSeedExplorer.buildCoreCollocates (lines 137-155) rescans all seed collocate lists for every common collocate in a nested stream: `seedCollocateMap.values().stream().flatMap(List::stream).filter(wsr -> c.equals(wsr.lemma()))`. For K common collocates and N total collocate results this is O(K*N), avoidable with a pre-built Map<String, List<Double>> populated during fetchCollocatesPerSeed

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition (lines 282-301) manually walks bracket depth with its own `depth++/depth--` loop to count tokens before a label, while countPatternTokens (line 257) already delegates to CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens for the same bracket-group counting. The two approaches diverge without clear reason, creating a subtle inconsistency

Main.java repeats a structurally identical switch-on-args[i] loop with `++i` inside each case across three separate command handlers (handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand, handleBlackLabServerCommand). There is no shared arg-parser helper, so each handler reimplements the same idiom independently
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern has a spurious double blank line (after the early-return guard for empty seedRelations at line 181) that interrupts the phase-by-phase rhythm of the method body

ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-JSON translation for exploration results; HTTP handlers call populateExploreResponse/populateComparisonResponse and do not inline collocate formatting

ExplorationService interface provides a minimal, honest seam (4 methods) between the HTTP layer and the exploration implementation; ExplorationHandlers depends only on the interface, not the concrete class
Options DTOs (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) make boundary inputs explicit and typed rather than passing loose primitive bags
GrammarConfigSerializer is a dedicated translation layer so GrammarConfig/RelationConfig remain pure data carriers with no JSON dependency
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a uniform, predictable error-propagation wrapper that every route registration uses consistently
resolveRelationConfig in ExplorationHandlers checks isEmpty() then calls orElseThrow() on line 265 — the terminal orElseThrow is unreachable dead code that obscures the intent

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges has a runtime branch: if perSeedCollocates is non-empty use it, else fall back to iterating seeds × collocates — this backward-compat fork means two structurally different ExplorationResult shapes flow through the same assembler path silently

handleSemanticFieldComparison assembles the response skeleton (status, seeds, seed_count, parameters) inline rather than delegating to buildCoreExploreResponse — asymmetric with the two sibling explore handlers that share a common helper

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples assembles the examples list inline with HashMap/ArrayList, a pattern that ExploreResponseAssembler standardised for exploration responses but was not extended to cover concordance responses

JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations used consistently on all public method parameters and return types across the codebase (QueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, ExplorationHandlers, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, ExplorationService interface).

Records used for all data-carrying types: WordSketchResult, SnippetResult, CollocateResult, HitRecord, ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, RelationConfig — providing compile-time field-name and type guarantees.
ConcordanceResult is a sealed interface with `permits SnippetResult, CollocateResult`, enabling exhaustive pattern-matching and compiler-enforced hierarchy.

Optional used correctly for nullable lookup returns: GrammarConfig.relation() returns Optional<RelationConfig>; RelationConfig.relationType() returns Optional<RelationType>; never Optional.get() without a check.

All HTTP response bodies use Map<String, Object> — ubiquitous in ExploreResponseAssembler, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers. Typed model objects (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, etc.) exist upstream but are immediately flattened to untyped maps for JSON serialisation. The outbound API shape is structurally invisible to the compiler.

VisualizationHandlers defines a RenderMode enum with a single value SIGNED, but the mode flows downstream as a raw String (RadialPlot.renderFromItems signature: `String mode`). The enum validates presence/absence but is never used as a typed enum value — textbook enum bypass pattern.

RelationConfig.name() and RelationConfig.description() are both @Nullable. SketchHandlers calls obj.put("name", rel.name()) and obj.put("description", rel.description()) without null-guards in four separate call sites (lines 90, 91, 113, 218, 263, 264), while GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson guards both with `if (rel.name() != null)`. Inconsistent null-safety at the JSON boundary.

RelationType enum carries four legacy/dead values (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) documented as 'Not set in grammar config'. They remain valid enum constants but no grammar config ever produces them. This creates a semantic mismatch: the type system permits these values in method signatures that conceptually exclude them.

CollocateQueryHelper no-arg constructor stores null in a BlackLabIndex field with no @Nullable annotation on the field. The design is intentional (testing seam) and documented, with Objects.requireNonNull guards in each method, but the field's declared type provides no nullability information to static analysis tools.

Domain vocabulary is precise and consistent throughout: 'lemma', 'collocate', 'logDice', 'deprel', 'xpos', 'bcql', 'headword', 'seed' mirror NLP terminology exactly, with no synonyms competing for the same concept across files.

CollocateProfile record has a primary field named 'adjective' (CollocateProfile.java:13) while the class header says 'Profile of one collocate across all seed nouns'. The field name 'adjective' leaks the initial adjective-specific design of the comparison endpoint into what became a generic collocate-profile type.

ComparisonResult.java uses 'adjectives' as the private field name (line 16) but exposes it through a public accessor named 'collocates()' (line 31). The same class's Javadoc, summaryCounts Javadoc, and method bodies freely mix 'adjective' and 'collocate' (e.g. 'Returns counts for fully-shared, partially-shared, and specific adjectives', variable 'CollocateProfile a' in loops). ExploreResponseAssembler serialises this list under the JSON key 'adjectives'.

DiscoveredNoun record (DiscoveredNoun.java:14) uses the field name 'combinedRelevanceScore', which is the sum of per-collocate logDice scores. ExploreResponseAssembler.formatDiscoveredNouns (line 118) serialises the same value under the key 'similarity_score'. Two different terms for the same quantity across the model and wire format creates a vocabulary jump when tracing from API output to model.

Handler names in SketchHandlers are specific and communicative: 'handleSurfaceRelations', 'handleDepRelations', 'handleFullSketchForType', 'handleDepSketch'. The pattern is consistent and descriptive.

Private helper methods in SemanticFieldExplorer name phases explicitly — 'fetchSeedCollocates', 'buildNounToCollocatesMap', 'filterByMinShared', 'identifyCoreCollocates' — matching the Javadoc phase descriptions.
Private record types name their concepts clearly: ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, ExplorationParams, SeedCollocateData, ExecutedSketch.

QueryExecutor interface method names use the 'execute' prefix consistently and differentiate precisely: executeCollocations, executeBcqlQuery, executeCqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern — each name signals the query mechanism used.
QueryResults.java uses a sealed interface (ConcordanceResult permits SnippetResult, CollocateResult) — exhaustive typing for concordance results with no open extension.
jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied consistently across public APIs: QueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, RelationConfig, ExplorationHandlers, ExploreResponseAssembler.
RelationType and PosGroup are enums used in switch expressions (RelationPatternBuilder.buildCollocateReversePattern, ComparisonResult.summaryCounts), not string constants.
Records used for all value types: WordSketchResult, CollocateProfile, CoreCollocate, BcqlRequest, ExplorationParams, ConcordanceExamplesRequest — immutability enforced by type.

Optional<RelationType> is a component field inside the RelationConfig record (line 31), violating Java's design intent that Optional is a return type only. Javadoc acknowledges the asymmetry but does not justify the field usage specifically.
All exploration handler methods return Map<String, Object> response bodies (56 occurrences across the api package) — the response shape is entirely untyped at compile time.

strengths: Package names map one-to-one to domain capabilities (exploration, query, config, indexer, api, model) with no historical-accident packages; a new engineer can state each package's purpose in one sentence.

API layer is split into fine-grained single-responsibility handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) rather than one monolithic server class.

Config subsystem explicitly separates data carriers (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig), loading logic (GrammarConfigLoader), and serialization (GrammarConfigSerializer) — each class has exactly one reason to change.
ExplorationService interface decouples the HTTP layer from the implementation, enabling test injection and insulating the API surface from exploration algorithm changes.

Delegation is explicit and documented: SemanticFieldExplorer's Javadoc names which responsibilities it owns vs. delegates (MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator), making the coordination model inspectable.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated rather than systemic: a single role-ambiguity in SemanticFieldExplorer (facade-that-is-also-algorithm-owner) and a single doc/runtime mismatch in the legacy route claim — both concentrated in boundary descriptions rather than pervasive structural rot.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The top-level decomposition is domain-aligned and predictable; ownership is clear across all major subsystems and a new engineer can navigate the codebase without surprises. Two concrete problems pull the score below 80: SemanticFieldExplorer carries two change axes (wiring and single-seed algorithm) whose coupling is masked by accurate-but-incomplete documentation, and the legacy-path Javadoc asserts reachability for /api/semantic-field that the route table never registers — a concrete reference-doc/runtime-reality gap. These are contained issues in an otherwise coherent architecture, placing the score solidly above 60 (no significant systemic drag) but short of 80 (uneven in exactly these two spots).

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper cleanly separates the two phases of BCQL execution (hit collection and scoring) with named intermediate records, making data flow through the pipeline explicit and testable

BlackLabSnippetParser is a well-bounded, side-effect-free utility: every method is a pure function of its input, patterns are compiled as class-level constants, and null handling is consistent throughout

GrammarConfigLoader decomposes a multi-step JSON validation into small, well-named private helpers that each do exactly one validation concern, keeping the top-level parse() method a readable orchestration
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a uniform, local error-mapping layer that prevents exception-handling logic from scattering across all handler classes
ExploreResponseAssembler achieves clean separation between model-to-presentation translation and HTTP concerns, with symmetric method pairs for the two exploration response shapes
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are isolated to three distinct spots — a quadratic inner scan in MultiSeedExplorer, a duplicated bracket-counting implementation in GrammarConfigLoader, and a repeated arg-parsing idiom in Main — rather than a systemic pattern, suggesting localised lapses in helper reuse rather than a broad structural problem.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase reads clearly and has genuine low-level elegance in most modules: helper extraction is balanced, control flow is direct, and state mutation is well-bounded. The recurring issues (quadratic re-scan, bracket logic reimplementation, arg-parsing repetition) are real but affect specific callsites rather than representing a pervasive style. This places the code solidly above the 60-level 'significant drag' band; the consistency of good patterns in the majority of the codebase outweighs the handful of localised defects. A score in the mid-70s reflects code that is genuinely well-structured but not yet at the 80+ 'solid throughout' level, held back by the missed helper-reuse opportunities that a careful review would flag.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler is a single, explicit translation seam: all model-to-response mapping for exploration lives there, not scattered across handlers
ExplorationService interface is minimal and honest — four methods that map directly to the four HTTP endpoint concerns, with no leakage of implementation details
Typed options DTOs (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) make boundary inputs explicit; no multi-parameter loose primitives crossing the HTTP→exploration seam
GrammarConfigSerializer correctly separates JSON-serialization concern from the config data model, keeping GrammarConfig and RelationConfig dependency-free
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides uniform error-propagation across all route registrations with a single, predictable pattern
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated inconsistencies in boundary discipline rather than structural seam failures: a silent legacy-compat fork in ExploreResponseAssembler, a redundant Optional unwrap in resolveRelationConfig, and asymmetric inline assembly in handleSemanticFieldComparison versus its sibling handlers.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The handoffs across the API→exploration and config→API boundaries are intentionally designed and mostly clean: the ExplorationService interface, ExploreResponseAssembler, and options DTOs are all good seam design. The codebase sits in the 'solid but uneven' range (80=solid) rather than exemplary (95+) because three real friction points exist: the silent dual-path in buildExplorationEdges degrades transparency at a boundary, the comparison handler assembles its own skeleton rather than using the shared builder, and the redundant orElseThrow in resolveRelationConfig is a minor but visible seam smell. These are all fixable without architectural change, which limits the severity, placing the score comfortably in the low-to-mid 70s.

strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied systematically across all public method signatures and record fields — static analysis tools have meaningful signal throughout the codebase.
Records used for all data-carrying DTOs (WordSketchResult, HitRecord, RelationConfig, ExplorationOptions) giving compile-enforced field names and types with no setters.
ConcordanceResult sealed interface with explicit permits clause enables exhaustive pattern matching and compiler-enforced hierarchy integrity.
Optional used correctly for query-result accessors (GrammarConfig.relation, RelationConfig.relationType) with no raw Optional.get() calls observed without prior presence checks.
type_safety.issue_character: Issues split between a single systemic pattern (Map<String, Object> for all response body construction, spanning every handler class) and two isolated local lapses (RenderMode enum bypass, @Nullable field accessed without guard); the systemic issue is common in Java REST codebases but is a real type-discipline gap, not an oversight.
type_safety.score_rationale: The domain model layer is genuinely well-typed: records, sealed interfaces, JSpecify annotations, and correct Optional use place it well above the 60-range codebases that skip annotations or rely on runtime nullchecks. What pulls the score down from the 85+ tier is the universal collapse to Map<String, Object> at the response-assembly boundary — the compiler sees nothing of the JSON shape, and typed response objects could easily be defined for each endpoint. The RenderMode bypass and inconsistent nullable-field guards are smaller deductions. Overall this sits comfortably in the solid-but-uneven 70–75 band: strong on the input/model side, weak on the output side.

strengths: Domain-specific NLP vocabulary (lemma, collocate, logDice, deprel, bcql, headword, seed) is used precisely and consistently across all packages with no synonym drift.

Phase-labelled private methods in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) name algorithmic phases and map directly to the documented exploration steps.

Handler classes and their public methods communicate both the resource domain and HTTP concern: ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples, ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore, VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial — no generic 'handle' or 'process' verbs without qualification.
Private record types used as local parameter containers are named as first-class concepts: BcqlRequest, ConcordanceExamplesRequest, ExplorationParams, SeedCollocateData, ExecutedSketch.
QueryExecutor interface method names use the 'execute' prefix consistently and differentiate query mechanisms precisely rather than using overloaded generic names.
naming_quality.issue_character: The naming issues are localised to the exploration model layer and stem from a single root cause: the comparison subsystem was originally designed for adjective collocates and then partially generalised, leaving 'adjective' vocabulary (CollocateProfile.adjective, ComparisonResult.adjectives field, Javadoc phrases, JSON key) in tension with 'collocate' vocabulary (the public CollocateProfile record name, ComparisonResult.collocates() accessor, broader codebase convention). A secondary model-to-wire vocabulary mismatch (combinedRelevanceScore vs similarity_score) compounds this in a small area.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase is solidly above average on naming: domain vocabulary is rich and consistent, method names communicate intent precisely, and there are no generic process/handle/do anti-patterns without qualification. The score is held back from the high-80s by the 'adjective'/'collocate' vocabulary split that runs through CollocateProfile, ComparisonResult, CollocateProfileComparator, and the response assembler — a developer reading these files encounters two competing terms for the same concept, which requires mental disambiguation. The combinedRelevanceScore/similarity_score mismatch is a secondary issue. Both are confined to the exploration model subsystem rather than being systemic across the codebase, placing this at a solid 78: noticeably better than a codebase with widespread naming fog, but short of the 80s where issues are truly isolated single occurrences rather than a recurring vocabulary split.

strengths: sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits clause gives exhaustive, compiler-enforced typing for the concordance result hierarchy
@NonNull/@Nullable from jspecify applied consistently across the public API boundary — rare in many Java codebases of this size
Enums (RelationType, PosGroup, SharingCategory, RelationEdgeType) used throughout instead of string constants, enabling switch exhaustiveness checks
Java records adopted uniformly for all value/DTO types, ensuring immutability and proper equals/hashCode without boilerplate
Optional used correctly as method return type for query-style accessors (GrammarConfig.relation, GrammarConfig.version, RelationConfig.relationType)
type_discipline.issue_character: Issues are isolated rather than systemic: one antipattern (Optional as record field) is contained to a single file, and the untyped Map<String, Object> response building is a pragmatic API-layer pattern rather than a deep structural failure.
type_discipline.score_rationale: The codebase shows more type discipline than most Java projects of comparable size — sealed types, consistent nullability annotations, enums, and records are all present and well-applied. Two issues pull the score down: Optional<RelationType> as a RelationConfig record component violates the Java Platform's stated design intent for Optional (return-type only), and 56 Map<String, Object> response-body constructions across the api package erase response shape at compile time. These are real discipline lapses, but they are limited in scope: the core domain model and query layer are strongly typed. On the 100=exemplary / 80=solid-but-uneven / 60=significant-drag scale, this lands just below solid-but-uneven — strong in the domain model and query layer, with the one conceptual violation in RelationConfig and the type-erasing response pattern holding it back.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer is documented as a coordination facade but owns the complete single-seed exploration algorithm, blurring its change boundary
issues.suggestion: Extract the single-seed exploration algorithm into a dedicated SingleSeedExplorer class mirroring the MultiSeedExplorer pattern. SemanticFieldExplorer then becomes a pure coordination facade with three one-liner delegates, its stated design fully matching its implementation and making each class's change boundary unambiguous.
evidence: Javadoc says: 'This class serves as the stable API surface for the HTTP exploration handlers: it owns the single-seed exploration algorithm directly … and acts as a coordination facade for the remaining operations — delegating multi-seed exploration to MultiSeedExplorer and profile comparison to CollocateProfileComparator.'

The class contains four private phase-helper methods (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) totalling ~130 lines of algorithmic logic, making it both a facade and a substantial algorithm implementation.

Multi-seed and comparison are fully delegated (one-liner forwards to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator) while single-seed is not — the delegation pattern is applied inconsistently across the three exploration modes.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc claims the legacy path /api/semantic-field is registered but the route table never binds it
issues.suggestion: Either remove the 'Also reachable at the legacy path /api/semantic-field' sentence from the Javadoc (if the legacy route was intentionally dropped), or add a registerGetHandler call for /api/semantic-field mapping to handleSemanticFieldComparison (if the legacy compatibility is still required). The current state makes reference docs untrustworthy for this endpoint.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc: 'Also reachable at the legacy path /api/semantic-field.'

WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes() registers /api/semantic-field/compare, /api/semantic-field/explore, /api/semantic-field/explore-multi, and /api/semantic-field/examples — but no /api/semantic-field context is created.
The class-level Javadoc endpoint table in WordSketchApiServer also omits any /api/semantic-field entry, confirming the legacy path is not active.

issues.summary: MultiSeedExplorer.buildCoreCollocates re-scans all collocate lists per common collocate (O(K*N))
issues.suggestion: In fetchCollocatesPerSeed, build a Map<String, List<Double>> (collocate lemma → per-seed logDice scores) alongside the existing collocateSharedCount. Pass it into buildCoreCollocates and use it directly instead of re-scanning seedCollocateMap.
evidence: Lines 144-155: for each c in commonCollocates, avgLd is computed by streaming all seedCollocateMap.values(), flatMapping to individual results, and filtering by lemma equality — a full O(N) scan per collocate
The collocate-to-logDice mapping is already available from fetchCollocatesPerSeed; a pre-built Map<String, List<Double>> accumulated there would reduce this to O(1) lookup per collocate

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition reimplements bracket-depth counting already provided by CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens
issues.suggestion: Refactor deriveTokenPosition to use CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens: split the pattern into tokens, find the index of the token whose raw string starts with the label prefix (e.g. '1:['), and return that index + 1. This removes the manual bracket-walking and keeps the two callers aligned.
evidence: countPatternTokens (line 257) delegates to CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens(pattern).size() for token counting

deriveTokenPosition (lines 282-301) manually walks the pattern string with its own depth++ / depth-- loop to count complete bracket groups before the label position, duplicating the bracket-group logic from CqlUtils

issues.summary: Three command handlers in Main.java each reimplement the same switch-on-args[i] with ++i idiom without a shared helper
issues.suggestion: Introduce a simple ArgumentParser inner class or record that wraps the args array and exposes a next(flag) method, or at minimum extract a parseCommonArgs(args, startIndex, Map<String,Consumer<String>>) helper that takes a map of flag → setter and handles the loop/error pattern once.
evidence: handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand, and handleBlackLabServerCommand each contain a for-loop over args with a switch statement and inline ++i; the structure is nearly identical across all three
Common boilerplate: requireNextArg is extracted, but the surrounding loop body (error reporting for unknown options, null-checking, early return on missing required args) is duplicated verbatim

issues.summary: Silent dual-path in ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges allows two different ExplorationResult shapes without making the distinction explicit at the boundary
issues.suggestion: Remove the no-perSeedCollocates constructor from ExplorationResult and require all callers to supply the per-seed map; delete the legacy fallback branch in buildExplorationEdges. If backward compat is required short-term, at minimum throw in the fallback branch rather than silently executing incorrect multi-seed edge attribution.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler line 41: `if (!perSeed.isEmpty()) { ... } else { // Legacy fallback }` — the assembler silently handles two structurally different ExplorationResult instances
ExplorationResult exposes two public constructors: one with perSeedCollocates and one without (defaults to Map.of()), making the 'legacy' shape easy to produce accidentally
The legacy fallback duplicates seeds × collocates in a cartesian product that is semantically wrong for multi-seed results where each seed has distinct collocate sets

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison assembles the response skeleton inline instead of using buildCoreExploreResponse, breaking symmetry with sibling handlers
issues.suggestion: Have handleSemanticFieldComparison call buildCoreExploreResponse (or a variant) for the shared response scaffold, passing Map.of("relation", "all") as extraParams; this keeps the three handlers symmetric at the response-building seam.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldComparison (line ~141): `response.put("status", "ok"); ... paramsUsed.put("relation", "all"); ...` — four response fields assembled inline
handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both delegate to buildCoreExploreResponse for the same fields
The comparison handler notes in its Javadoc that it 'intentionally does not share the seed/relation parsing preamble' but this doesn't justify the response assembly asymmetry

issues.summary: resolveRelationConfig checks isEmpty() then calls orElseThrow() — the terminal unwrap is unreachable and obscures the error-propagation contract
issues.suggestion: Replace the isEmpty check + orElseThrow pattern with a single `orElseThrow(() -> new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown relation: " + relationId))`, then check relationType on the unwrapped value directly. This makes the error-propagation path readable in one pass.
evidence: Lines 257-265: `if (relationConfig.isEmpty()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException(...) } ... return relationConfig.orElseThrow();` — the orElseThrow on line 265 can never trigger because the isEmpty check on line 257 already throws
A reader must trace both paths to confirm the orElseThrow is dead code, adding unnecessary cognitive load at the method's return seam

issues.summary: All HTTP response bodies built as Map<String, Object> despite typed upstream model objects existing
issues.suggestion: Introduce typed response records (e.g. ExploreResponse, EdgeDto, SketchRelationResponse) for each endpoint family, serialise them with FastJSON or Jackson. This makes the outbound API shape compiler-checkable and removes the silent-typo risk in map key strings.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler.serializeEdge returns Map<String, Object> with keys 'source', 'target', 'log_dice', 'type' — shape is invisible to the compiler.
ExplorationHandlers.buildCoreExploreResponse constructs and returns Map<String, Object> with fields 'status', 'relation_type', 'parameters' inserted manually by string keys.
ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse mutates a passed Map<String, Object> — callers cannot see which fields were set without reading the implementation.
CorpusQueryHandlers.buildBcqlResponse returns Map<String, Object> with 6 manually keyed fields despite the upstream BcqlRequest being a typed record.

issues.summary: RenderMode enum defined but bypassed: mode flows to RadialPlot as raw String, never converted to enum type
issues.suggestion: Change RadialPlot.renderFromItems to accept a @Nullable RenderMode parameter; convert the validated string to the enum before calling renderFromItems, or pass null when mode is absent. This makes the valid values compiler-enforced end-to-end.
evidence: VisualizationHandlers line 70: `if (mode != null && !mode.isEmpty() && !RenderMode.isValid(mode))` validates the string but never converts it.
RadialPlot.renderFromItems signature is `String mode` (line 145 of RadialPlot.java) — takes the raw string, so the enum provides no compile-time guarantee about what values are passed.
RenderMode has a single value SIGNED with a static isValid() string comparison method — pattern consistent with an enum that was added for type safety but whose type is not used downstream.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.name() and description() are @Nullable but accessed without null-guard in SketchHandlers
issues.suggestion: Either add null-guards in SketchHandlers to match GrammarConfigSerializer, or promote name/description to @NonNull with a validation step in GrammarConfigLoader (since missing names would likely be a config error). The inconsistency itself signals the annotation is not being enforced at call sites.
evidence: RelationConfig record declares `@Nullable String name` and `@Nullable String description` (lines 23–24).
SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType (lines 90–91): `obj.put("name", rel.name()); obj.put("description", rel.description())` — no null guards.
SketchHandlers.buildRelationResponse (lines 263–264): same unguarded access pattern for description.
GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson correctly guards both: `if (rel.name() != null) obj.put("name", rel.name())` — demonstrating the design intent but inconsistent application.

issues.summary: CollocateProfile.adjective field name conflicts with the type's own 'collocate' vocabulary across the exploration model
issues.suggestion: Rename CollocateProfile.adjective to CollocateProfile.collocate (and update toString, all callers). Rename ComparisonResult.adjectives field to collocates to match its public accessor. Update variable names in CollocateProfileComparator (adj → colloc or collocate) and ExploreResponseAssembler (the JSON key 'adjectives' → 'collocates'). This aligns the model, accessor, wire format, and collaborator variable names under a single term.
evidence: CollocateProfile record (CollocateProfile.java:13) declares its primary field as 'adjective', while the record's own Javadoc says 'Profile of one collocate across all seed nouns'.

ComparisonResult (ComparisonResult.java:16) stores the list as 'private final List<CollocateProfile> adjectives' but exposes it via 'public List<CollocateProfile> collocates()' (line 31) — the same data has two names in the same file.

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse puts the list under JSON key 'adjectives' (line ~186) and calls formatCollocateProfile, which in turn reads 'adj.adjective()' — so the wire format, the accessor, and the formatter all use different vocabulary.

CollocateProfileComparator uses variable 'adj' (package-local) and 'adjectives' (local map key) consistently with the original design, but the public API exposes 'collocate', creating a within-module vocabulary split.

issues.summary: DiscoveredNoun.combinedRelevanceScore is serialised as 'similarity_score' — model and wire-format names convey different semantics
issues.suggestion: Pick one term and use it consistently. Since the value is the sum of logDice scores (a relevance ranking, not a normalised similarity), rename either: (a) rename the JSON key to 'relevance_score' to match the model, or (b) rename the model field to 'similarityScore' if the intention is to communicate semantic closeness to API consumers. Option (a) is lower risk; option (b) requires updating sorting comparators and toString.
evidence: DiscoveredNoun record (DiscoveredNoun.java:14) declares 'double combinedRelevanceScore'; SemanticFieldExplorer.filterByMinShared (line 256) sets it as the sum of per-collocate logDice scores.

ExploreResponseAssembler.formatDiscoveredNouns (line 118) serialises the same field as 'nm.put("similarity_score", MathUtils.round2dp(n.combinedRelevanceScore()))' — 'combined relevance' (a ranking quantity) becomes 'similarity' (a semantic-distance concept) in the JSON output.
Callers sort by combinedRelevanceScore (SemanticFieldExplorer.java:196) treating it as a ranking score, while the 'similarity_score' JSON key implies a bounded normalised similarity.

issues.summary: Optional<RelationType> used as a RelationConfig record component instead of as a return type only
issues.suggestion: Change the record component to `@Nullable RelationType relationType` (using jspecify annotation which is already imported), and expose it through an accessor method `public Optional<RelationType> relationType() { return Optional.ofNullable(this.relationType); }`. This follows the Java Platform's intent (Optional as return type) and keeps the stored value a plain nullable reference. Update GrammarConfigLoader and all callers of relationType() — the change is source-compatible since Optional<RelationType> is already the public return type.
evidence: RelationConfig.java line 31: `Optional<RelationType> relationType` is a record component — Optional is serialised, stored in maps, compared, and passed as a value throughout the codebase.
The Javadoc 'nullability design note' explains deriveDeprel()'s null return but does not address why the field itself uses Optional instead of @Nullable RelationType.
GrammarConfigLoader calls relObj.getString("relation_type") which can return null — the correct fix is to store @Nullable RelationType and expose Optional via the accessor method.
